critic_raw:
YNet's two-stage prediction architecture first predicts a goal distribution, then conditions the path prediction on sampled waypoints. This design introduces a compounding error structure: errors in goal prediction propagate into waypoint sampling, which then propagate into path prediction. The evaluation reports end-to-end metrics (ADE/FDE) that aggregate over this entire chain, making it impossible to determine whether performance improvements come from better goal modeling, better waypoint-conditioned path modeling, or simply better tuning of the stochastic sampling procedure. Without stage-wise ablation metrics, the architectural attribution claim cannot be verified.

The Stanford Drone Dataset (SDD) and ETH/UCY have well-documented train/test splits, but SDD in particular has a severe scene imbalance problem: different drone-captured scenes have very different pedestrian densities, speeds, and interaction patterns. A model that performs well on SDD by effectively memorizing scene-specific appearance priors (encoded in the segmentation maps) cannot be claimed to have learned generalizable trajectory forecasting — it may have learned scene-specific shortcuts. The segmentation map input is a significant source of this risk because it gives the model direct access to scene geometry, and for well-represented scenes, this is a strong distributional hint.

The multi-modal prediction evaluation using ADE and FDE is standard but biased toward methods that generate concentrated predictions. FDE measures the minimum distance among K sampled trajectories to the ground truth endpoint, which means a method can improve FDE simply by generating more diverse samples and relying on minimum-of-K coverage. If the number of trajectory samples K is not held constant across all compared methods, FDE comparisons are invalid. The paper claims to 'outperform existing approaches' without specifying whether K is matched.

The segmentation map input introduces a preprocessing dependency that is rarely discussed as a source of variance: segmentation quality affects trajectory prediction quality, but the evaluation reports only trajectory metrics. If the segmentation model used at evaluation time differs from what baselines assume, or if segmentation errors on unseen scenes are higher than on training scenes, performance on new environments will degrade in ways the reported metrics do not capture.
all_issues_raised: The two-stage architecture compounds goal-prediction errors with path-prediction errors, but only end-to-end ADE/FDE are reported; without stage-wise ablation, the performance contribution of each stage cannot be isolated.

SDD's scene imbalance means a model with access to segmentation maps can learn scene-specific appearance shortcuts rather than generalizable trajectory dynamics; the evaluation does not separate in-distribution scene performance from cross-scene generalization.

FDE is evaluated as minimum-of-K sampled trajectories, and if K is not held constant across compared methods, methods with larger K gain a trivial coverage advantage that inflates reported FDE improvements.

Segmentation map quality is a preprocessing dependency that introduces variance not captured in trajectory metrics; degraded segmentation on unseen environments would cause silent performance drops not reflected in the benchmark numbers.
all_issues_adjudicated: The two-stage architecture compounds goal-prediction errors with path-prediction errors, but only end-to-end ADE/FDE are reported; without stage-wise ablation, the performance contribution of each stage cannot be isolated.

SDD's scene imbalance means a model with access to segmentation maps can learn scene-specific appearance shortcuts rather than generalizable trajectory dynamics; the evaluation does not separate in-distribution scene performance from cross-scene generalization.

FDE is evaluated as minimum-of-K sampled trajectories, and if K is not held constant across compared methods, methods with larger K gain a trivial coverage advantage that inflates reported FDE improvements.

Segmentation map quality is a preprocessing dependency that introduces variance not captured in trajectory metrics; degraded segmentation on unseen environments would cause silent performance drops not reflected in the benchmark numbers.
